sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Neuroblastoma is a cancer that originates from the developing sympathetic nervous system and is the most common extracranial solid tumor in children [1,2]. Due to the heterogenous nature of neuroblastoma—with some patients experiencing spontaneous regression and others requiring intensive multimodal therapy—a risk-adapted, multidisciplinary approach is essential. Approximately 600 to 800 patients are diagnosed annually in the United States, with an average age at diagnosis of 1 to 2 years, and the vast majority are <5 years of age [3,4]. Treatment is stratified by risk group (low, intermediate, or high) based on the International Neuroblastoma Risk Group (INRG) Risk Classification System, which incorporates age at diagnosis, INRG stage, MYCN amplification status, histopathology, segmental chromosomal aberrations (SCA), and DNA index (DI) [47]. The goal of therapy for low- and intermediate-risk patients is to cure disease with minimal toxicity, while high-risk patients require intensive multimodal therapy divided into induction, consolidation, and post-consolidation phases [MS-10]. The NCCN Neuroblastoma Panel recommends participation in clinical trials when available and applicable [NEUROB-D 3 of 13].

nodes.label: Individualized Therapy (Chemoimmunotherapy, MIBG, targeted agents, etc.)

cards.title: Curative
cards.detail: Surgical resection or observation with surveillance, depending on INRG stage and biologic features [NEUROB-3]. For patients with L1 tumors, surgery is often curative. Infants <6 months with L1 adrenal tumors ≤3.1 cm solid or ≤5 cm cystic may be observed without biopsy [NEUROB-3, NEUROB-D 1 of 13].

cards.title: Curative
cards.detail: Combined moderate-intensity multiagent chemotherapy (2–8 cycles based on risk factors) and surgical resection, with treatment tailored to disease biology and response [NEUROB-4–6, NEUROB-D 1 of 13].

cards.title: Curative
cards.detail: Intensive multimodal therapy including induction chemotherapy, surgical resection, high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue (tandem transplant recommended for most patients, category 1), consolidative radiation therapy, post-consolidation anti-GD2 immunotherapy with isotretinoin, and optional continuation therapy with eflornithine [NEUROB-7–11, NEUROB-D 3 of 13].

cards.title: Disease control / palliative
cards.detail: Individualized therapy including chemoimmunotherapy (anti-GD2 antibody with chemotherapy), I-131 MIBG, targeted agents for actionable alterations (e.g., ALK inhibitors), radiation, surgery, or clinical trials [NEUROB-D 10 of 13].
